Q1: Being 28 and having been out of formal education for a while, I had to be pretty deliberate about my study routine. I typically carved out time in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings, dedicating around 23 hours a week when I could manage it. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often record myself reading notes aloud or listen to lecture recordings while doing other tasks. The structure helped me feel in control, even when the material got challenging.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was coming up. I tried to maintain some consistency with weekend study blocks, but when deadlines approached, everything else went out the window and I'd cram in extra hours wherever I could find them. Work responsibilities and my extracurricular commitments sometimes threw my schedule off completely, forcing me to adapt week by week.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew an assignment was due or when I remembered there was new content posted. Sometimes I'd get a reminder from a classmate in our discussion group, which would prompt me to check in. Without reliable internet at home, I had to plan my LMS access around when I could get to the library or a café with wifi, so it wasn't always spontaneous.
Q4: Honestly, it never quite became automatic for me because of the internet access issue. I'd have these windows where I could get online, and I'd try to batch everything together—downloading materials, checking discussions, submitting work. Over time, I developed a rhythm of going to the library every few days, so in that sense it became part of my weekly routine, just not a daily habit.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me because I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the concepts auditorily. The discussion forums were also helpful—I posted quite a bit and interacted with peers, which helped clarify things I was confused about. Having that community aspect made me feel less isolated as an older student returning to education.
Q6: I tended to skip over the reading-heavy PDF documents unless absolutely necessary. My learning style just doesn't click with dense text, and I'd find myself zoning out. Some of the optional quizzes I also delayed because they felt like busywork when I was already struggling to keep up with the core assignments and exam prep.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to the assessments or if it helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. The discussions where people shared different perspectives on problems—those felt valuable because I learned from hearing others think through the material. If something felt disconnected from what I needed to pass, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they carried this mental weight of 'nice to have' rather than 'must do.' When I was anxious about my exam performance, I couldn't justify spending time on optional stuff when required work was piling up. Required activities had urgency attached to them; optional ones felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford.
Q9: Absolutely. Since I don't have consistent internet at home, I downloaded everything I could during my library sessions—lecture recordings, PDFs, assignment instructions. Then I'd study from those files offline for hours, listening to recordings on my phone during my commute or while cooking. A huge portion of my actual learning happened away from any connected device.
Q10: Most of my studying happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes I'd made. I'd spend entire evenings working through problems on paper, talking through concepts out loud to myself, or listening to recorded lectures. None of that would show up in the LMS—it would just look like I wasn't doing anything when really I was putting in serious hours.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video once, then watch it three or four times offline over the following week. Same with assignment rubrics—I'd print them out and annotate them by hand as I worked. The LMS would only register that single download, not the repeated engagement that actually happened afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was scared—genuinely scared—that I wasn't going to pass despite all my effort. Work got busier, and I felt this mounting anxiety that made it difficult to focus even when I had time to study. The mindfulness exercises they recommended helped a bit, but there were definitely weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage properly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities, my LMS usage dropped to just the bare minimum—checking deadlines, submitting what was due. The fatigue made it hard to do the deeper engagement like participating in discussions or watching supplementary videos. I had to triage constantly, and the LMS often got the short end.
Q14: There were times I'd open a video and let it play while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about the exam or about work stuff. I'd technically be 'watching' but absorbing nothing. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what anyone was saying. My body was there, but my brain wasn't.
Q15: I postponed practice quizzes repeatedly even though I knew they'd help with the exam. Part of it was fear—I was scared to see how poorly I might do and have that confirm my worst anxieties. It was easier to avoid them and tell myself I'd do them 'when I was more prepared,' but that time never really came.
Q16: Deadlines were essentially the engine that drove my LMS activity. I'd have these bursts of intensive logging in and submitting right before things were due. Without a deadline, I'd often let the LMS sit untouched for days. The structure of due dates gave me external accountability that I desperately needed to stay on track.
Q17: When I got feedback, it definitely affected my next steps. Positive comments motivated me to engage more; critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and hesitant to submit the next thing. I appreciated detailed feedback because it gave me something concrete to work on, but vague comments left me feeling uncertain about what to change.
Q18: The weekly module structure helped me know what to expect and when. Having clear deadlines and a predictable rhythm made it easier to plan my limited online time. But when modules had too many components or unclear instructions, I'd get overwhelmed and fall behind, which then snowballed into more anxiety.
Q19: The discussion forums being active made engagement easier—having peers to interact with kept me connected to the course. What made it harder was the assumption that everyone had constant internet access. Some activities required real-time participation or frequent check-ins that were difficult for me to manage with my connectivity situation.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand but struggling with the material. They might be logging in repeatedly, rewatching videos, reading discussions—all trying to grasp concepts—but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or can't translate that effort into completed work. Fear of failure can paralyze you even when you're putting in the hours.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. A lot of my learning happened offline, so my LMS activity was lower than my actual engagement. Someone could download materials once, study extensively from those downloads, and perform decently on assessments without the logs showing much. The LMS only captures a fraction of what students actually do.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accesses materials, submission patterns, and participation in discussions. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone was actually focused or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work, the emotional state of the student, and whether life circumstances are affecting their ability to engage.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just completing tasks. It's about curiosity, asking questions, connecting ideas to what you already know. For me, it also means feeling part of a learning community—interacting with peers, feeling like my contributions matter. It's both cognitive and emotional investment in the course.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, how many times I re-listened to lectures on my phone, or the hours I spent handwriting notes. It wouldn't capture my anxiety before exams or the fear that kept me up at night worrying about failing. It also wouldn't show the effort I put into the mindfulness practices to manage that anxiety, or how much being an older student shaped my experience.
